items.q: What is the sum of the exterior angles of any convex polygon?
items.correct: 360 degrees — regardless of the number of sides.
items.misconception: It depends on the number of sides — more sides means a larger sum.
items.random_wrong: 180 degrees, the same as a triangle's interior sum.
items.why: Students assume exterior angle sum scales with the number of sides (like the interior sum), missing that it is invariant at 360 degrees.

items.misconception: 14, because you read and calculate left to right (3 + 4 = 7, then 7 x 2).
items.random_wrong: 9, because you add all the numbers and then subtract the operator count.
items.why: Students compute left-to-right by default, ignoring the precedence rules in BIDMAS/BODMAS.

items.correct: -9 — without brackets, the exponent applies only to the 3, then the negative is applied: -(3^2) = -9.
items.misconception: 9, because squaring a negative number always gives a positive.
items.why: Students don't distinguish -3^2 from (-3)^2; the first is -9 because squaring binds tighter than the unary minus, but the second is +9.
It's reject H₀. “accept H₀” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: rejecting H₀ when it is true
items.misconception: accepting H₀ when it is false
items.why: Correct answer: rejecting H₀ when it is true. “accepting H₀ when it is false” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: At α = 5%, what is the critical p-value threshold (give as a decimal)?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.05. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
